claim: Workflows allow code to pause and resume execution indefinitely across multiple days with zero cost when not computing, fundamentally changing how serverless orchestration works for long-running tasks
actionable: Use workflow patterns for AI agent orchestration where waiting on human approval or external events incurs no cost; implement pause-resume for multi-step tasks
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Webhook-based human-in-the-loop workflows can be implemented simply: generate an ephemeral webhook URL, store in database, have users trigger approval which resolves the awaited webhook
actionable: Implement human approval loops in workflows using simple webhook patterns instead of complex approval systems; generate unique URLs for each approval request
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Never ship abstractions you haven't used yourself in production (dogfooding); frameworks built without this principle tend to be wrong or over-engineered for real-world use
actionable: Use the tools and abstractions you build before shipping; extract them from working applications rather than designing them theoretically
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Stay low-level in framework abstractions when the problem space is emerging (AI apps); high-level abstractions too early lead to rewriting when trends shift (e.g., chatbots to agents)
actionable: Build flexible, low-level primitives for AI frameworks rather than high-level opinionated abstractions; avoid Hollywood principle (don't call us, we call you) for immature domains
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Streaming is critical for AI apps (models take 30 seconds); frameworks that make streaming easy unlock significant developer value where traditional web development treats sub-500ms latency as optional
actionable: Build streaming-first abstractions in AI frameworks; prioritize end-to-end streaming from model to user interface for responsive AI interactions
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Anomaly detection has a recall-precision tradeoff: tune too aggressively and you get false alarms waking oncall engineers; tune conservatively and you miss real issues. Agents solve this by running slowly with full context
actionable: Use AI agents for investigation and triage of anomalies with access to time series, logs, and context rather than relying on tuned alerting rules alone
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Agents are extraordinarily effective for specific problems (meeting notes, UI changes, lead qualification) but ineffective for others; finding the 'sweet spot' requires asking what people hate most about their jobs
actionable: Identify high-impact agent use cases by surveying employees on tedious, repetitive tasks they dislike; target problems that are boring and manual but require judgment
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Agents for lead qualification, abuse analysis triage, and data warehouse queries are mature use cases; agents should not yet make firewall changes or DNS migrations (too risky)
actionable: Use agents for information gathering and pre-work; reserve final decisions on critical infrastructure changes for human approval
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Vercel is investing in Python support with zero-config deployments (Flask, FastAPI) and fluid compute pricing (pay only when computing); AI backends often run 30+ second responses making free idle time valuable
actionable: Use Vercel's fluid compute for Python AI backends where you only pay during active compute; build async patterns that benefit from idle-time-free infrastructure
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Building secure apps assumes incompetent developers and incompetent AI; infrastructure should enforce security independent of app quality (extract auth, minimize data scope in app)
actionable: Design applications and infrastructure layers that prevent data exposure and privilege escalation even if the application code is poorly written or AI-generated
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: AI agents work well when given tools for querying time series, analyzing logs, and accessing source code; they synthesize findings to make probabilistic decisions better than tuned rules
actionable: Equip agents with tools for observability (queries, logs, code access) rather than just single-purpose APIs; let agents synthesize multi-modal context
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl

claim: Forward-deployed engineers help large companies identify high-impact agent use cases and build the first 1-2 agents; the goal is to empower them to build agents independently by the 3rd
actionable: Structure agent adoption programs with hands-on support for early use cases; gradually reduce support to enable independent agent development
source_title: Ship AI recap: Agents, Workflows, and Python — w/ Vercel CTO Malte Ubl
